title: Large-Scale Quantum Circuit Simulation on an Exascale System for QPU Benchmarking
abstract: Recent advances in quantum computing have enabled the development of quantum processors with hundreds of qubits. However, noise continues to limit the amount of useful information that can be extracted from these systems, making it essential to identify the regime in which experimental outputs remain reliable. In this work, we benchmark Quantinuum Helios-1, a 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum processing unit, using the linear ramp quantum approximate optimization algorithm (LR-QAOA). To this end, we perform large-scale noiseless simulations on JUPITER, Europe's first exascale supercomputer, for circuits of up to 48 qubits and 3,384 two-qubit gates. These simulations, executed on 4,096 nodes equipped with 16,384 GH200 superchips and high-bandwidth CPU-GPU interconnects, provide a reference for validating experimental results at the edge of classical tractability. We find that, up to 48 qubits, Helios-1 remains in a noise-tolerant region, i.e., its samples cannot be clearly distinguished from those coming from a noiseless simulation. We then extend the analysis to larger system sizes using experimental data only, and apply a mean-of-means resampling procedure with a 3$σ$ threshold to determine whether the QPU output is statistically distinguishable from random sampling. This analysis identifies a regime of coherent performance up to 93 qubits (12,834 two-qubit gates), beyond which, at 95 qubits, the outputs become statistically indistinguishable from random sampling. These results demonstrate how exascale classical simulation can be used to validate quantum processors, and provide a quantitative boundary between noise-tolerant and random regimes in quantum processors.
summary: This paper presents a large-scale benchmarking of the 98-qubit Quantinuum Helios-1 trapped-ion processor. By utilizing Europe's first exascale supercomputer, JUPITER, the researchers performed massive noiseless simulations to provide a reference for quantum hardware performance. They successfully identified the transition point where quantum processor outputs become indistinguishable from random noise, finding that coherent performance persists up to 93 qubits. This work establishes a vital framework for validating large-scale quantum computers at the limits of classical simulation capability.
main_problem: Identifying the boundary between the noise-tolerant and random regimes in large-scale quantum processors and validating hardware performance at the edge of classical tractability.
main_result: The Quantinuum Helios-1 processor maintains coherent performance up to 93 qubits, with outputs becoming statistically indistinguishable from random sampling at 95 qubits.
method: The study uses the Linear Ramp Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (LR-QAOA) for benchmarking, combined with large-scale noiseless simulations on the JUPITER exascale supercomputer.
model_or_system: The experimental platform is the Quantinuum Helios-1, a 98-qubit trapped-ion QPU using a QCCD architecture with 137Ba+ hyperfine qubits. The problem model is the Weighted Max-Cut (WMC) on fully connected graphs.
key_observables: Approximation ratio (r), overlap (r_ovl), and statistical distinguishability from random sampling using a mean-of-means resampling procedure.
important_parameters: Qubit counts (up to 98), circuit depth (p), number of two-qubit gates (up to 12,834), and the 3-sigma threshold for noise classification.
main_assumptions: The benchmarking assumes a non-variational, platform-agnostic protocol (LR-QAOA) to avoid classical optimization complexities and uses a uniform depolarizing error model for analysis.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) shows the simulation of the LR-QAOA approximation ratio versus circuit depth under varying noise levels; Figure 3 illustrates the time scaling of the simulations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the LR-QAOA benchmarking framework, describes the experimental and exascable simulation platforms, details the statistical methodology for regime classification, presents the performance results for Helios-1, and concludes with a hardware-specific cost and scaling analysis.
why_interesting: It provides a quantitative boundary for the utility of NISQ devices and demonstrates how exascale classical computing can be used to validate the reliability of large-scale quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Noise in LR-QAOA. (a) Simulation of LR-QAOA under different levels of depolarizing noise as a function of circuit depth. (b) Overlap between the noisy and noiseless approximation ratios for 8-, 10-, and 12- qubit simulations. The solid lines correspond to the prediction from Eq. 8. Three algorithm performance regimes are highlighted with boxes.

Fig. 2. Sampling distribution for the 40-qubit LR-QAOA benchmark at p = 3. KEDs of the mean approximation ratio ¯r are shown for the random sampler at 10 and 100 shots, and for the noiseless simulation at 10 shots. The dashed line indicates the Helios-1 QPU result.

Fig. 3. Time scaling of LR-QAOA circuits on JUPITER. (a) Runtime as a function of problem size. (b) Strong scaling for the 40-qubit problem. (c) Hardware performance comparison between H100 (JUPITER) and A100 (JUWELS Booster) GPUs. (d) Performance comparison between JUPITER and JUWELS Booster for a 40-qubit problem as a function of LR-QAOA depth.

Fig. 4. LR-QAOA (p = 3) benchmarking of Helios-1 approximation ratio vs. number of qubits. (a) Comparison with noiseless classical simulations on JUPITER and with the previous-generation Quantinuum QPUs (H2-1). (b) Helios-1 experiments for problem sizes beyond the reach of exact JUQCS simulations on JUPITER. The solid dark green line indicates the true approximation ratio r.(c) Number of two-qubit gates as a function of problem size. The green shaded region and dashed line indicate the largest system size for which Helios-1 samples remain statistically distinguishable from random sampling.
